title: Understand: Federalism: Examples Throughout American History
description: Check your understanding of federalism throughout American history with this set of multiple-choice questions.
topic_path: Understand: Federalism: Examples Throughout American History

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which types of policy are—at least, in part—responsibilities of the states?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**Which statement best describes how the issue of slavery was handled by different levels of government prior to the Civil War?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: States were allowed to decide whether to outlaw slavery or not.
States had to get formal approval from the national government to maintain the institution of slavery.
The federal government had no power over the institution of slavery.
The federal government outlawed slavery with an amendment to the Constitution.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which statement best describes the purpose of the Fugitive Slave Act?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It allowed slaveholders or their agents to cross state lines to capture and retrieve people who escaped from enslavement.
It banned slavery from extending into territories in the western half of the United States.
It detailed the process through which people who were formerly enslaved could become citizens.
It freed all enslaved people from captivity if they resided in southern states.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which law was passed specifically to limit the effectiveness of the Fugitive Slave Act?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**Which strategies were used by suffragists to secure women’s right to vote?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Women sought the right to vote in some elections at the state level when they couldn’t get a federal law or amendment passed.
Women petitioned Congress to ratify a constitutional amendment granting them the right to vote.
Women protested in front of the White House to pressure the president to support women’s suffrage.
Women brought lawsuits arguing that the Fourteenth Amendment already protected their right to vote as a privilege of American citizenship.
Women organized bus boycotts and held sit-ins at restaurants and lunch counters to draw attention to their cause.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which statement best describes the division of powers between the federal government and states over elections?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: States are primarily responsible for overseeing and regulating elections, though the federal government can regulate some aspects of federal elections.
States can regulate only state and local elections, while the federal government is primarily responsible for overseeing federal elections.
States have unlimited powers to regulate elections, and the federal government has no ability to limit that power.
States must provide local officials to handle elections, but any regulations must come from the federal government.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which level of government is primarily responsible for setting voter qualifications?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**Which section of the Constitution gives states the power to set voter qualifications in congressional elections?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

**Which section of the Constitution recognizes a state’s power to set voter qualifications for __state and local__ elections?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

**Which constitutional question emerged during the COVID-19 pandemic?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Which level of government was responsible for dealing with problems caused by the pandemic?
Should the government require that every person has to wear a mask in public spaces?
Why did the COVID-19 virus spread so quickly throughout the United States?
Should employers allow employees to work from home to avoid contracting the virus?

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which level of government was primarily responsible for determining the approach schools should take during the COVID-19 pandemic? **

[[☃ radio 1]]
